agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby 2025 Annual Municipal Report presented to Council
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to receive and consider the 2025 Annual Municipal Report and a shorter Highlights version. This report summarizes the city's finances, services, and achievements for the past year. It is a proposed overview for council's information and discussion, not a final decision or new law. No immediate changes to business rules or fees are proposed in this item.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.1
2025 ANNUAL MUNICIPAL REPORT AND ANNUAL MUNICIPAL REPORT HIGHLIGHTS
Purpose: To present the 2025 Annual Municipal Report and 2025 Annual Municipal Report Highlights.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to replace Development Procedures Bylaw
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal to repeal the current Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw (2022) and replace it with a new Development Procedures Bylaw (2026), effective July 1, 2026. The changes would also update the Burnaby Building Bylaw and the Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw. The new bylaw consolidates all development applications (including new development permit types and sign permits), formalizes fee and refund practices, and expands delegated authorities. It is required to implement the new Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 and updated Development Permit Area Guidelines.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision on May 12, 2026 — if approved, new permit rules and fees take effect July 1, 2026.

key_dates.label: Council vote on proposed Development Procedures Bylaw 2026
New Development Procedures Bylaw 2026 takes effect

agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.2
PROPOSED REPEAL AND REPLACEMENT OF BURNABY DEVELOPMENT PROCEDURES BYLAW AND RELATED AMENDMENTS TO THE BURNABY BUILDING BYLAW AND BURNABY CONSOLIDATED FEES AND CHARGES BYLAW
Purpose:
To seek Council approval to repeal
Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw, 2022
and replace it with the proposed
Development Procedures Bylaw, 2026
, to amend the
Burnaby
Building Bylaw, 2016
and
Burnaby
Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw.

attachments.title: Proposed Repeal and Replacement of Burnaby Development Procedures Bylaw and Rela

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to apply for award on development approval changes
agenda_briefs.summary: Council will consider endorsing an application for a 2026 UBCM Community Excellence Award for the Development Approvals Process (DAP) program. This is a proposed update on Phase 3 of the DAP program, which aims to make getting development permits faster and simpler. The decision is not final yet — council will vote on whether to apply for the award.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.3
DAP PROGRAM - PHASE 3 FINAL REPORT
Purpose: To provide an update on the activities and outcomes of the Development Approvals Process (DAP) program and seek endorsement to apply for the 2026 UBCM Community Excellence Awards.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract for mobile devices and plans
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract award for the supply and delivery of mobile devices, services, subscription plans, and other data services. If approved, this would authorize the city to buy new mobile phones, tablets, and related service plans for city staff. The decision is up for approval at an upcoming council meeting and has not yet been finalized.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.4
CA - MOBILE DEVICE AND PLANS
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the supply and delivery of mobile devices, services, subscription plans, and other data services.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Council to vote on contract for aeration and overseeding services
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract for aeration and overseeding services. This proposed contract would cover work on city-owned parks, sports fields, and other green spaces. If approved, the contract would pay a company to punch small holes in the soil (aeration) and spread grass seed (overseeding) to keep the turf healthy. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider and vote on this at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.5
CA - AERATION AND OVERSEEDING SERVICES
Purpose:
T
o obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for aeration and overseeding services.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on contract for Victory St. road upgrades
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on awarding a contract for road upgrades on Victory Street, between Patterson Avenue and Jubilee Avenue. If approved, this would fund construction work to improve the road. The decision is on the agenda for an upcoming council meeting and has not yet been finalized.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.6
CA - (CB-3233) VICTORY ST. - PATTERSON TO JUBILEE ROAD UPGRADES
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the Victory St. – Patterson to Jubilee Road Upgrades project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Council to vote on sidewalk contract for Triumph, Carleton, Rosser, Gilmore, and Madison
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a contract award for a sidewalk package project on Triumph, Carleton, Rosser, Gilmore, and Madison streets. If approved, this would authorize the city to hire a contractor to build new sidewalks in these areas. The decision is not final yet — it will be decided at the upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: No action required from you at this time. Watch for the council decision — it could affect street access or parking near these locations.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.7
CA - (CA-3299) TRIUMPH, CARLETON, ROSSER, GILMORE, AND MADISON SIDEWALK PACKAGE
Purpose: To obtain Council approval for a Contract Award (CA) for the Triumph, Carleton, Rosser, Gilmore, and Madison Sidewalk Package project.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Contract change for Burnaby sidewalk expansion program
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposed contract change for the Sidewalk Expansion Program (EB-8022). This change would adjust the existing contract for building new sidewalks. If approved, it could affect construction timelines and costs for sidewalk projects in the city. The decision has not been made yet — Council will consider this at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.8
CC - (EB-8022) SIDEWALK EXPANSION PROGRAM
Purpose:
To obtain Council approval for a Contract Change (CC) to the (EB-8022) Sidewalk Expansion Program.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider comment letters on Metro 2050 land-use amendments
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to consider sending comment letters to the Metro Vancouver Regional District about two proposed changes to the Metro 2050 regional growth strategy. One amendment, proposed by the City of Maple Ridge, would change land-use designations on North 256 Street. The other, proposed by the City of Surrey, would change designations in the Hazelmere area. These are site-specific proposals that could affect future development and land use in those areas. Council will decide whether to forward Burnaby's comments on these proposed amendments.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
7.9
COMMENT LETTERS ON PROPOSED METRO 2050 AMENDMENTS: CITY OF MAPLE RIDGE (NORTH 256 STREET) and SURREY (HAZELMERE)
Purpose:
To inform Council about two different site-specific Metro 2050 Amendments, one proposed by the City of Maple Ridge and the other by the City of Surrey, and to consider forwarding comment letters to the Metro Vancouver Regional District.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to review street width standards
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a motion that would direct city staff to review the City's street width standards. This means the city would look at how wide streets need to be for new developments. A proposed change could affect how much land is used for roads versus other uses like sidewalks, bike lanes, or green space. This is not a final decision yet — it is a first step to study the issue.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.1
COTW - PROPOSED WORKS AND SERVICES REQUIREMENTS
Purpose: To direct staff to review the City's street width standards.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on consultant budget cuts for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposal that would require staff to prepare a full list of consultant budget cuts for 2026. The proposal asks staff to identify which consultant contracts will be cut, which departments are affected, and which consultants will keep their funding. This is not a final decision yet — council must approve the plan before any cuts take effect. If approved, this could mean fewer outside experts hired by the city next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, it could affect businesses that provide consulting services to the city.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.2
FMC - 2026 CONSULTANT FUNDNG AND BUDGET REDUCTIONS
Purpose: To seek Council approval for staff to provide a comprehensive list of consultant budget cuts, including associated functions and departments, and a separate list of consultants retaining funding for 2026.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to vote on proposed Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to consider and vote on a proposed Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan. This plan would set new rules for how trails on Burnaby Mountain are used, maintained, and managed. If approved, it could affect access for hikers, mountain bikers, and other trail users. The plan is not yet in effect — Council is being asked for direction on the draft at this stage.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the Council decision — if approved, new trail rules could affect your business or recreation use.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.3
PRCC - DRAFT BURNABY MOUNTAIN TRAIL MANAGEMENT PLAN
Purpose: To provide an update and seek Council direction on the Draft Burnaby Mountain Trail Management Plan.

agenda_briefs.title: Council to consider supporting Vancouver Roques Rugby's Bingham Cup 2028 bid
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a request from Vancouver Roques Rugby to support their bid to host the Bingham Cup in 2028. The Bingham Cup is an international rugby tournament for gay and inclusive teams. Council will consider whether to provide a letter of support or other backing for the bid. This is a proposed decision — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
8.4
PRCC - VANCOUVER ROQUES RUGBY - BINGHAM CUP 2028 SUPPORT REQUEST
Purpose: To seek Council support for Vancouver Roques Rugby's bid to host the Bingham Cup in 2028.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Non-market housing project at Norland Avenue
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby Council is set to vote on a proposed non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue. Non-market housing means homes that are rented or sold at below-market rates, often run by a non-profit or government agency, to make them more affordable. This is an update on the proposal — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
9.1
PROPOSED NON-MARKET HOUSING PROJECT AT 3961 AND 3983 NORLAND AVENUE
Purpose: To provide an update on the proposed non-market housing project at 3961 and 3983 Norland Avenue.

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 would replace 1965 rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed new zoning bylaw for Burnaby. The Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026 would repeal the old 1965 Zoning Bylaw and replace it with updated rules. This includes new Development Permit Area Guidelines (rules for getting a permit to build or develop) and a reassignment of zoning districts (changing what you can do on your property). If approved, this would affect what you can build, where you can build it, and how you can use your land. The first reading of the bylaw is on the agenda — this is the first step in the approval process.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for public hearing dates — this is your chance to comment on how the new rules affect your property.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.1
#14815 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to repeal Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 and replace it with the proposed Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026, including new Development Permit Area Guidelines and zoning district reassignment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to update enforcement bylaw for new zoning rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would update how the city enforces zoning penalties. The amendment would replace an old reference to the 1965 Zoning Bylaw with a reference to the new 2026 Zoning Bylaw. This is a technical change to make sure the city can still issue tickets and fines under the updated zoning rules. If passed, it will affect anyone who owns property or runs a business in Burnaby and must follow zoning rules.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, this change will affect how zoning penalties are enforced under the new 2026 bylaw.
agenda_briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.2
#14816 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 4, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (repeal Schedule A, Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965 section and replace with Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 2026)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby to amend affordable housing fee waivers
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would change the definitions of 'affordable housing' and 'Zoning Bylaw' in the city's rules for waiving Amenity Cost Charges (ACC) and Development Cost Charges (DCC). These charges are fees developers pay to help cover the cost of community amenities like parks and infrastructure. If approved, the new definitions could affect which housing projects qualify for fee reductions or waivers, potentially impacting development costs and the types of housing built in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect your development project's eligibility for fee waivers.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.3
#14817 - Burnaby Waivers and Reductions of Amenity Cost Charges and Development Cost Charges Bylaw 2024, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Waivers and Reductions of ACC and DCC Bylaw 2024 (definitions of "affordable housing" and "Zoning Bylaw")

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026 — first reading
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on the first reading of a proposed Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026. This proposed bylaw would create new rules to protect tenants whose homes are being redeveloped. If passed, it could require landlords to give tenants more notice, help with moving costs, or offer the right to return to a new unit at a similar rent. Council will consider this proposal at an upcoming meeting — it has not been approved yet.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this could affect your rights as a tenant or your obligations as a landlord in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.industries: Tenant, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Construction & Development
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.4
#14821 - Burnaby Tenant Protection Bylaw 2026
Purpose: to establish protections for tenants impacted by redevelopment

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw amendment to strengthen tenant protection enforcement
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed amendment to Burnaby's Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw. The change would update how the city enforces rules that protect tenants. If approved, the new rules would give the city more tools to make sure landlords follow tenant protection laws. This is not yet law — it is up for discussion and a vote at an upcoming council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council meeting date and consider attending or submitting comments if you are a landlord or tenant in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.5
#14822 - Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009, Amendment Bylaw No. 6, 2026
Purpose: to amend the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 (tenant protection)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby mid-year fee and charge increases for 2026
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would increase many of the fees and charges the City of Burnaby collects from businesses and residents. This is a mid-year update to the city's main fees bylaw. If approved, the new rates would take effect later in 2026. The exact fees affected are listed in the proposed bylaw (Amendment Bylaw No. 3, 2026). Business owners should check the agenda to see if their specific licence, permit, or service fees are going up.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Review the proposed fee changes in the agenda to see if your business's licence or permit fees are affected.

key_dates.label: Council vote on proposed Burnaby fee bylaw amendment

agenda_briefs.industries: Liquor, Cannabis, Construction & Development, Food Service, Hotel & Accommodation, Spa & Wellness, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.1.6
#14823 - Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw, Amendment Bylaw No. 3, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Consolidated Fees and Charges Bylaw (2026 mid-year amendments)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby e-bike and e-scooter pilot bylaw up for final vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on final adoption of a bylaw that would amend Burnaby's Street and Traffic Bylaw to create a pilot program for e-bikes and electric kick scooters. If approved, this would set new rules for where and how these devices can be used on city streets and paths. The decision has not been made yet — this is the final step before the pilot becomes law.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, new rules for e-bikes and e-scooters could affect your delivery or transportation operations.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.2.1
#14811 - Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1961, Amendment Bylaw No. 1, 2026
Purpose: to amend Burnaby Street and Traffic Bylaw 1964 (e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program)

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby e-bike pilot parks regulation bylaw amendment
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on final adoption of a bylaw that would amend the Burnaby Parks Regulation Bylaw 1979. This proposed change would allow e-bikes and electric kick scooters in certain city parks as part of a pilot program. If approved, the new rules would let you ride these electric devices on designated park paths and trails, but not on all park areas. The pilot program is meant to test how e-bikes and scooters work in parks before making any permanent rules. The bylaw amendment is up for final approval at the next council meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, check which park trails are open to e-bikes before riding.

key_dates.label: Burnaby e-bike pilot parks bylaw final adoption vote

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby bylaw to enforce e-bike and e-scooter pilot rules
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on a proposed bylaw that would give the city new enforcement powers for its e-bike and electric kick scooter pilot program. If approved, this amendment to the Burnaby Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw 2009 would allow the city to issue tickets and fines for violations like improper parking, riding on sidewalks, or exceeding speed limits. The pilot program is currently testing how these vehicles can be safely used on city streets, and this enforcement bylaw is the next step to make sure riders follow the rules. The decision has not been made yet — council will consider the bylaw at an upcoming meeting.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if passed, this bylaw could affect any business that uses e-bikes or e-scooters for deliveries or operations in Burnaby.
agenda_briefs.source_body:

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby property tax rates for 2026 up for final vote
agenda_briefs.summary: Council is set to vote on final adoption of the Burnaby Rates Bylaw 2026. This proposed bylaw would set the tax rates on all taxable land and buildings in the city for the 2026 tax year. The rates are based on sections 197 and 216 of the Community Charter, which is the provincial law that gives municipalities the power to set property taxes. If approved, this bylaw will determine how much property owners pay in municipal taxes next year.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — this will directly affect your 2026 property tax bill.
agenda_briefs.industries: Landlord & Property Mgmt, Retail, Professional Services

agenda_briefs.title: Proposed: Burnaby council to vote on abandoning Grand Promenade rezoning
agenda_briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council is set to vote on a proposal to abandon a rezoning bylaw for a large development at 3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858 and 9898 Gatineau Place. The developer wants to change the approved plan, which included two residential towers (80 and 73 storeys) plus a 13-storey retail and office podium. The new proposal would remove the office space and add 292 more market strata residential units (condos you can buy). Council will decide whether to scrap the old rezoning and consider this new plan.
agenda_briefs.action_required: Watch for the council decision — if approved, this could affect nearby property values and development plans.
agenda_briefs.source_body:
11.3.1
#14737 - Burnaby Zoning Bylaw 1965, Amendment Bylaw No. 6, 2025 - REZ #24-23 (3900 Grand Promenade, 9850 Austin Road, and 9858 and 9898 Gatineau Place)
Purpose: to amend the previous rezoning of the subject lands pursuant to REZ #18-32 (Bylaw No. 14447), which received Final Adoption on September 23, 2024, and included two market strata residential buildings of 80 and 73 storeys, respectively, inclusive of a 13-storey retail and office podium. The applicant is proposing to remove commercial office use and replace it with additional market strata residential use, which includes the proposal of 292 additional market strata units
